title: Odd spin symmetry and anisotropy switching in p-wave magnet CeNiAsO
authors: Fayuan Zhang, Huaxun Li, Xingkai Cheng, Yibo Fan, Yifan Yin, Yifan Gao, Zhanfeng Liu, Shengtao Cui, Zhouyi Yin, Yue Zhao, Junhao Lin, Zhengtai Liu, Mao Ye, Yaobo Huang, Shan Qiao, Wu Xie, Ping Miao, Hao Wu, Junwei Liu, Guanghan Cao, Chaoyu Chen
abstract: Odd-parity magnets, complementary to altermagnets, exhibit unique properties such as high efficiency in charge-spin conversion and compatibility with conventional superconductivity, of critical importance in the pursuit of energy-efficient spintronics and topological superconductors for quantum computation. For even-parity d-wave and g-wave altermagnets, the magnetic structure, spin-split band structure and physical properties are currently under intensive study. On the contrary, while hundreds of odd-parity magnets and the promising properties have been predicted in theory, experimental studies are scarce. Specifically, the magnetic structure and transport properties of candidates NiI2 and Ga3Ru4Al12 have been reported, yet the characteristic band structure and particularly the odd-parity spin symmetry remain elusive. Here we demonstrate experimentally the deterministic p-wave spin symmetry and resistance anisotropy switching for the prototype odd-parity magnet, CeNiAsO. Angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) reveals two cleaved terminations with distinct surface band structure. By compensating the polar surface, we achieve intrinsic bulk band structure, for which the spin splitting can be well described by the p-wave magnetic structure through first-principles calculation. The bulk spin polarization measured by spin-resolved ARPES exhibits symmetry with only one degenerate plane, fingerprint of p-wave magnetism. We further demonstrate giant resistance anisotropy and switching between high-resistance and low-resistance states through modest field-induced domain selection, highlighting its potential for antiferromagnetic spin memory devices. The structural similarity between CeNiAsO and 1111-type Fe-based superconductors stimulates further exploration on the interplay between p-wave magnetism, superconductivity and band topology.
summary: This paper provides the first experimental evidence of p-wave spin symmetry in the odd-parity magnet CeNiAsO. By using potassium deposition to reveal intrinsic bulk bands, the authors demonstrate that magnetic domains can be manipulated to switch resistance anisotropy, suggesting high potential for antiferromagnetic spintronic memory devices.
main_problem: The experimental verification of intrinsic p-wave spin symmetry in odd-parity magnets and the connection between this symmetry and observed anisotropic transport properties.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate p-wave spin symmetry in CeNiAsO using spin-resolved ARPES and show that magnetic domain selection can switch the material between high- and low-resistance states.
method: The study combines spin-resolved ARPES, STM, and transport measurements on single crystals and FIB-fabricated devices, supported by first-principles DFT calculations and in situ potassium deposition to remove surface states.
model_or_system: The system is CeNiAsO, an antiferromagnetic Kondo metal with a layered tetragonal structure and p-wave magnetic order characterized by a non-relativistic spin group symmetry C4z^t.
key_observables: Spin polarization (asymmetry in EDCs), resistance anisotropy, magnetoresistance ratio, and Fermi surface topology.
important_parameters: Magnetic transition temperatures (TN1 approx 7.4 K, TN2 approx 9.4 K), magnetic field up to 9 T, and temperature regimes down to 2 K.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that potassium deposition effectively compensates for polar surface states to reveal the intrinsic bulk band structure.
figures_summary: Figures show XRD and EDS for composition, STM for surface termination identification, ARPES for Fermi surface and band spectra, and transport data showing resistance anisotropy and domain switching.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the importance of odd-parity magnets, details the structural and magnetic characterization of CeNiAsO, presents ARPES results showing p-wave symmetry after surface modification, demonstrates resistance anisotropy switching via magnetic field, and concludes with the implications for spintronics and superconductivity.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
